Title: Biosensor and machine learning-aided engineering of an amaryllidaceae enzyme

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Salmonella enterica subsp. enterica serovar Typhimurium
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Branchpoint intermediate for Amaryllidaceae alkaloid biosynthesis, including therapeutic alkaloids such as galantamine
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Engineered RamR variants enable sensitive and highly selective detection of 4′-O-methylnorbelladine in E. coli, supporting screening of pathway activity rather than directly altering metabolite biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Wild-type RamR showed weak responsiveness to both norbelladine and 4′-O-methylnorbelladine. 2. Directed evolution produced variant 4NB2.1 with approximately 2.5 μM detection limit and >80-fold preference for 4′-O-methylnorbelladine over norbelladine. 3. The evolved biosensor quantitatively tracked Nb4OMT activity and was ~10-fold more sensitive than the HPLC method used for comparison.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Directed evolution using SELIS; fluorescence dose-response assays; ligand docking; flow cytometry; comparison with HPLC
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutively expressed from plasmid in E. coli during biosensor assays
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Constitutive expression of RamR from pReg-RamR; cognate PramR promoter driving sfGFP reporter
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Induction with 4′-O-methylnorbelladine or norbelladine; growth in LB medium; tested mainly at 37°C
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: RamR is a biosensor scaffold and not a native Amaryllidaceae pathway gene. Core experimentally relevant variants included K63T/L66M in first round and additional C134D/S137G in 4NB2.1. Included as Common Gene because it is a metabolite-responsive regulator used as a screening tool, not a pathway TF controlling plant metabolism.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a high-throughput biosensor module for screening enzyme/pathway variants that increase 4′-O-methylnorbelladine production in microbial engineering platforms; can accelerate pathway optimization but is not itself a breeding target.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Does not directly raise metabolite production; performance depends on plasmid context, inducer range, and specificity window.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Daffodil (wild Narcissus sp. aff. pseudonarcissus / Narcissus pseudonarcissus as described in text)
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Plant O-methyltransferase; SAM-dependent methyltransferase; homodimeric enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Early common pathway leading to branchpoint intermediate 4′-O-methylnorbelladine
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Branch-point forming step / late step in the known early common pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to the 4′-hydroxyl of norbelladine to form 4′-O-methylnorbelladine
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control/bottleneck enzyme for microbial 4′-O-methylnorbelladine production in this study due to low activity and imperfect regiospecificity of wild type enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Central branchpoint intermediate for therapeutic Amaryllidaceae alkaloids such as galantamine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Wild-type Nb4OMT enables 4′-O-methylnorbelladine biosynthesis in E. coli from supplied norbelladine; engineered variants improve titer, catalytic efficiency, thermostability, and regioselectivity while reducing 3′-O-methylnorbelladine off-product formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Heterologous expression of Nb4OMT in E. coli converted norbelladine into 4′-O-methylnorbelladine, measurable by biosensor and HPLC. 2. Wild-type enzyme was catalytically inefficient and generated a 3′-O-methylnorbelladine off-product. 3. MutComputeX-guided variant A53M increased product titer by ~60%, raised kcat/Km by about 2-fold via >2.1-fold higher kcat, decreased residual substrate ~17-fold, reduced off-product ~3-fold, and increased Tm by 1.7°C. 4. Triple variant E36P/G40E/A53M further improved thermostability to 58.4°C, about 5.6°C above wild type.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in E. coli; biosensor-linked fluorescence assay; HPLC; LC-MS; enzyme kinetics; DSF; X-ray crystallography
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutive plasmid expression in E. coli; fluorescence correlated with expression strength
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Supplemented norbelladine substrate; temperatures 25°C, 30°C, and 37°C; 37°C improved product formation; Ca2+ included in kinetic/crystallographic context
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Wild-type enzyme showed substrate inhibition evidence in improved variants at high substrate concentration. Crystal structure of engineered Nb4OMT E36P/G40E/A53M deposited as PDB 8UKE. A53M benefit depended on inclusion of ligand/cofactor atoms in structure-guided design. Source species is daffodil; text alternates between Narcissus pseudonarcissus and Narcissus sp. aff. pseudonarcissus.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for metabolic engineering of microbial Amaryllidaceae alkaloid production. Beneficial substitutions such as A53M and stabilizing E36P/G40E can be stacked to improve activity, specificity, and thermostability. Important enzyme to optimize before extending pathway toward galantamine.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Wild-type and engineered variants can show off-target 3′-O-methylation; improved variants showed substrate inhibition at high substrate levels; microbial performance depends on precursor supply.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: P150-RBS(riboJ) constitutive expression cassette on plasmid backbone
